name: How should I design storage for small coastal kitchens with limited wall space?

mainEntity.name: In small coastal homes in Pacifica or Half Moon Bay with lots of windows and few full walls, how can I still get enough kitchen storage in a remodel?
mainEntity.text: In small coastal homes in Pacifica or Half Moon Bay with lots of windows and few full walls, how can I still get enough kitchen storage in a remodel?

acceptedAnswer.text: Coastal homes in Pacifica and Half Moon Bay often prioritize views and light, leaving kitchens with many windows but few uninterrupted walls for cabinets. Effective storage design starts with maximizing the wall sections you do have: full‑height pantry units, stacked cabinets to the ceiling, and deep drawer bases instead of shelved bases. Narrow pull‑outs beside ranges, pantries, or fridges can hold spices, oils, and baking trays in just a few inches of space. Islands and peninsulas are critical storage engines in these layouts. Use cabinets on both the kitchen and seating sides where depth allows, and opt for drawers rather than doors wherever possible to avoid wasted back‑of‑cabinet space. Corner cabinets with pull‑out or rotating systems can reclaim otherwise dead corners. Above windows, consider shallow display or storage shelves if ceiling height permits, or short upper cabinets bridging between wall sections. Toe‑kick drawers can add hidden space for flat items like trays. Work with your contractor on a detailed cabinet plan that lists exactly what will be stored where—pots, small appliances, pantry items—before ordering. This approach ensures the design compensates for limited walls and supports how you actually cook and live in a view‑oriented home.
acceptedAnswer.description: In window‑heavy coastal kitchens, maximize vertical and hidden storage: full‑height pantry cabinets, deep drawer bases, narrow pull‑outs, and optimized corner solutions. Use islands or peninsulas with cabinets on multiple sides and consider shallow upper cabinets above windows where possible. Careful planning often replaces missing wall space with smarter storage zones.
